refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks clarity on the mechanisms by which efficiency is achieved. Refinement introduces the key enablers: monitoring (telemetry), control (operator/tele-operator actions), and feedback loops. This makes the requirement testable and decomposable into sub-functions that map to system capabilities.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over the evaluation period. Compare against E_target. Verify that the NGHE solution provides the necessary instrumentation, control interfaces, and feedback to support operator decision-making that achieves this target.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption and work output (tonnage moved) via telemetry.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback to the operator and tele-operator to enable informed control decisions.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The Vehicle shall execute energy-optimized motion control strategies (e.g., smooth acceleration, efficient load distribution) responsive to operator commands.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy and productivity data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system that monitors real-time power draw, calculates efficiency metrics, and broadcasts feedback to operator interfaces; couple with vehicle-level power optimization algorithms.
DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across vehicle sensors and tele-operation control logic; aggregate metrics at the operator interface layer only.
DP-C: Implement energy efficiency as a constraint in the vehicle's autonomous motion planning; provide operator with efficiency-aware recommendations rather than real-time feedback.

DP-D: Establish a closed-loop energy optimization loop: monitor consumption → calculate efficiency → adjust vehicle control parameters → feedback to operator; integrate across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Establish a closed-loop energy optimization loop that integrates monitoring (Vehicle sensors), calculation (centralized or distributed), control adjustment (Vehicle actuation), and feedback (Teleoperation interface). This approach ensures that energy efficiency is not a passive metric but an active control objective, enabling both operator-directed and system-assisted optimization.

potential_sources: Vehicle motion control must be tightly coupled with energy monitoring to enable responsive optimization.
Teleoperation interface must receive real-time efficiency feedback to inform operator decisions.
Safety Services must validate that energy optimization does not compromise safety constraints (e.g., braking performance, stability).
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP introduces necessary couplings between Vehicle control and Teleoperation feedback, but these are functional couplings (information flow and control loops) rather than undesirable physical or architectural couplings. The coupling is justified by the requirement to achieve efficiency through integrated monitoring and control.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle sensors can accurately measure energy consumption (power draw, fuel/battery state) with sufficient fidelity for real-time feedback.
Assumption: Operator and tele-operator can interpret and act on efficiency feedback within the time scales of vehicle operation.
Risk: If energy monitoring is delayed or inaccurate, feedback-driven optimization may be ineffective or counterproductive.
Risk: Closed-loop optimization may conflict with safety constraints if not properly validated; Safety Services integration is critical.
Risk: Network latency in Teleoperation may degrade the responsiveness of feedback-driven control.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the role of Safety Services in validating energy optimization strategies. Specify acceptable latency bounds for telemetry and feedback to ensure operator responsiveness. Define fallback strategies if energy monitoring becomes unavailable.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. At this level, the IM assignment reflects that energy efficiency is a system-level property that emerges from the coordinated operation of all sub-modules. The NGHE Solution IM owns the responsibility for defining and enforcing the energy efficiency KPI and ensuring that all sub-modules contribute to its achievement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (power draw, fuel/battery state), work output (tonnage moved, distance traveled), efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton), and vehicle status. Flows from Vehicle and aggregation logic to external monitoring systems and operator interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator and tele-operator commands that specify work objectives (e.g., move X tons of material from location A to B). Tasking may include efficiency preferences or constraints (e.g., 'minimize energy use' vs. 'maximize speed').

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services validates that energy optimization strategies do not violate safety constraints. SafetyAlert flows from Safety Services to NGHE Solution (constraints/alerts) and from NGHE Solution to Safety Services (proposed optimization actions for validation).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for Teleoperation, Telemetry, and SafetyAlert. Enables remote operation and monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Energy efficiency is a system-level KPI that requires integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Containment is high because the DP (closed-loop energy optimization) is realized through the coordinated operation of sub-modules, all of which are contained within NGHE Solution. Diffusion is minimal because the energy efficiency logic is not scattered across external systems.

Vehicle contains the energy monitoring sensors, power actuation, and motion control algorithms. However, energy efficiency optimization also requires feedback from Teleoperation (operator input) and validation from Safety Services. Thus, Vehicle alone cannot fully realize the DP; it is a critical component but not the sole container. Diffusion arises from the need to coordinate with Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Teleoperation provides the operator interface for receiving efficiency feedback and issuing control commands. However, Teleoperation does not contain the energy monitoring logic or vehicle control algorithms. It is a critical interface layer but not the primary container for the DP. Diffusion is significant because energy optimization logic is distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Safety Services validates that energy optimization does not compromise safety. However, Safety Services does not own the energy monitoring or optimization logic itself. Its role is to constrain and validate the optimization performed by Vehicle and Teleoperation. Diffusion is high because safety validation is a cross-cutting concern that affects multiple modules.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (system-level owner) with primary realization responsibility delegated to Vehicle (monitoring and control) and Teleoperation (feedback and operator interface), subject to Safety Services validation.
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency as a system-level KPI is best contained within the NGHE Solution boundary because it requires integration across Vehicle (monitoring and control), Teleoperation (feedback and operator interface), and Safety Services (constraint validation). No single sub-module can fully realize the DP in isolation. The NGHE Solution IM owns the responsibility for defining the energy efficiency target, orchestrating the closed-loop optimization, and ensuring that all sub-modules contribute to its achievement.
containment_risks: Risk: If energy monitoring logic is duplicated across Vehicle and Teleoperation, diffusion increases and consistency is compromised. Mitigation: Establish a single source of truth for energy metrics (e.g., centralized telemetry aggregation).

Risk: If Safety Services validation is not tightly integrated into the optimization loop, energy optimization may inadvertently violate safety constraints. Mitigation: Define explicit safety gates in the optimization logic and require Safety Services sign-off on optimization strategies.

Risk: Network latency in Teleoperation may degrade the responsiveness of feedback-driven control, reducing the effectiveness of operator-directed optimization. Mitigation: Specify acceptable latency bounds and implement local (vehicle-level) optimization strategies as a fallback.

Risk: Operator and tele-operator may not have sufficient training or situational awareness to interpret efficiency feedback and make optimal control decisions. Mitigation: Provide decision support (e.g., efficiency recommendations) and automated optimization strategies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit ownership and interface contracts between NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Define the energy efficiency orchestration logic at the NGHE Solution level, with clear delegation of monitoring (Vehicle), feedback (Teleoperation), and validation (Safety Services) responsibilities. Specify the Telemetry interface as the single source of truth for energy metrics to avoid diffusion.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (closed-loop energy optimization loop) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through integrated monitoring, control, and feedback). The DP specifies a logical solution approach that is testable and decomposable. The coupling between Vehicle control and Teleoperation feedback is justified by the requirement to achieve efficiency through integrated monitoring and control.

diffusion_risks: Energy monitoring logic must be centralized (e.g., in Vehicle or a dedicated telemetry aggregation module) to avoid duplication across Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Optimization algorithms must be clearly owned by either Vehicle (local optimization) or NGHE Solution (system-level orchestration) to avoid diffusion.
Safety validation logic must be owned by Safety Services and invoked as a gate in the optimization loop, not duplicated in Vehicle or Teleoperation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate container for the system-level energy efficiency KPI and orchestration logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are sub-modules that contribute to the realization of the DP. The containment hypothesis is sound, but explicit interface contracts and ownership boundaries are required to minimize diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through measurement of energy_per_ton over the evaluation period. The NGHE Solution IM provides the necessary interfaces (Telemetry for data collection, Tasking for operator input, SafetyAlert for constraint validation) to support verification. Verification requires integration testing across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of energy efficiency responsibility to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (monitoring and control), Teleoperation (feedback and operator interface), and Safety Services (constraint validation) are well-defined. However, explicit interface contracts and ownership boundaries must be documented to ensure clarity during detailed design.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit ownership and interface contracts between NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Define the energy efficiency orchestration logic at the NGHE Solution level.
Specify the Telemetry interface as the single source of truth for energy metrics to avoid diffusion and ensure consistency.
Define acceptable latency bounds for Telemetry and Tasking to ensure operator responsiveness and feedback-driven control effectiveness.
Establish safety gates in the optimization loop and require Safety Services sign-off on optimization strategies.
Clarify the role of operator and tele-operator in energy optimization (e.g., decision support vs. autonomous optimization).

decision.justification: The refined FR is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs that map to specific system capabilities (monitoring, feedback, control, and data aggregation). The selected DP is sound and addresses the refined FR, but its realization requires detailed design of the sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their interfaces. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership boundaries, interface contracts, and implementation strategies for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption and work output (tonnage moved) via telemetry.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback to the operator and tele-operator to enable informed control decisions.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The Vehicle shall execute energy-optimized motion control strategies (e.g., smooth acceleration, efficient load distribution) responsive to operator commands.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy and productivity data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

assumptions: Vehicle sensors can accurately measure energy consumption (power draw, fuel/battery state) with sufficient fidelity for real-time feedback.
Operator and tele-operator can interpret and act on efficiency feedback within the time scales of vehicle operation.
Network latency in Teleoperation is acceptable for feedback-driven control (latency bounds to be specified in detailed design).
Safety Services can validate energy optimization strategies without introducing unacceptable delays in the control loop.
Energy efficiency is a system-level property that emerges from the coordinated operation of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
open_risks: If energy monitoring is delayed or inaccurate, feedback-driven optimization may be ineffective or counterproductive.
Closed-loop optimization may conflict with safety constraints if not properly validated; Safety Services integration is critical.
Network latency in Teleoperation may degrade the responsiveness of feedback-driven control.
Operator and tele-operator may not have sufficient training or situational awareness to interpret efficiency feedback and make optimal control decisions.
Energy monitoring logic may be duplicated across Vehicle and Teleoperation, increasing diffusion and reducing consistency.
evidence_needed: Specification of acceptable latency bounds for Telemetry and Tasking interfaces.
Detailed design of energy monitoring sensors and algorithms in Vehicle.
Specification of energy efficiency feedback format and presentation in Teleoperation interface.
Definition of energy-optimized motion control strategies and their implementation in Vehicle.
Safety validation procedures for energy optimization strategies.
Operator training and decision support requirements for energy efficiency.
